title_en: Author Biography and His Dictionary of Eminent Men
title_id: Biografi Pengarang dan Kamus Para Tokoh Terkemukanya
content_en:
Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Ibrahim ibn Abi Bakr ibn Khallikan was born in Irbil (Erbil in modern Iraq) in 608 AH (1211 CE) into a family of Kurdish origin that claimed descent from the Barmakid family of the early Abbasid period. He received an excellent education in his native city and in Mosul before traveling to Syria and Egypt, where he eventually settled and pursued a distinguished judicial and scholarly career.

Ibn Khallikan served as a Shafi'i judge (qadi) in Damascus on multiple occasions — a position he first held under the Ayyubid sultan and later under the early Mamluk regime — and was recognized as one of the foremost legal scholars of his generation. His personal relationships with major political figures of the era, including the brilliant Ayyubid prince and scholar al-Malik al-Ashraf, gave him access to information and sources that enriched his biographical work.

His major work, Wafayat al-A'yan wa Anba Abna az-Zaman (Deaths of Eminent Men and News of the Sons of the Age), universally known as Wafayat al-A'yan or Ibn Khallikan's Biographical Dictionary, is one of the most celebrated biographical dictionaries in Islamic literary history. Composed over many years and completed in 672 AH (1274 CE), it contains entries for approximately 865 notable figures organized alphabetically.

Wafayat al-A'yan is distinguished from other Islamic biographical dictionaries by several qualities: the elegant Arabic literary prose in which it is written, the breadth of its scope (including scholars, rulers, literary figures, and others, not only hadith scholars), the literary quotations it preserves, and its inclusion of entertaining anecdotes alongside scholarly information. These qualities made it as much a work of adab (belles-lettres) as a scholarly reference.

Ibn Khallikan died in Damascus in 681 AH (1282 CE), leaving behind a biographical dictionary that has been read by scholars and educated readers across the Arab world ever since its composition.
content_id:
Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Ibrahim ibn Abi Bakr ibn Khallikan lahir di Irbil (Erbil di Irak modern) pada 608 H (1211 M) dalam keluarga berketurunan Kurdi yang mengklaim keturunan dari keluarga Barmakid pada periode Abbasiyah awal. Beliau menerima pendidikan yang sangat baik di kota kelahirannya dan di Mosul sebelum bepergian ke Suriah dan Mesir, tempat beliau akhirnya menetap dan menjalani karier peradilan dan keilmuan yang terhormat.

Ibn Khallikan bertugas sebagai hakim Syafi'i (qadhi) di Damaskus dalam beberapa kesempatan — jabatan yang pertama kali dipegangnya di bawah sultan Ayyubiyah dan kemudian di bawah rezim Mamluk awal — dan diakui sebagai salah satu ulama hukum terkemuka dari generasinya. Hubungan pribadinya dengan tokoh-tokoh politik utama di era tersebut, termasuk pangeran dan ulama Ayyubiyah yang brilian al-Malik al-Asyraf, memberinya akses ke informasi dan sumber-sumber yang memperkaya karyanya dalam biografi.

Karya utamanya, Wafayat al-A'yan wa Anba' Abna' al-Zaman (Wafatnya Para Tokoh Terkemuka dan Kabar Putra-Putra Zaman), yang secara universal dikenal sebagai Wafayat al-A'yan atau Kamus Biografi Ibn Khallikan, adalah salah satu kamus biografi paling termasyhur dalam sejarah sastra Islam. Disusun selama bertahun-tahun dan diselesaikan pada 672 H (1274 M), ia memuat entri-entri untuk sekitar 865 tokoh terkemuka yang disusun secara alfabetis.

Wafayat al-A'yan dibedakan dari kamus-kamus biografi Islam lainnya oleh beberapa kualitas: prosa sastra Arab yang elegan yang menjadi medium penulisannya, keluasan cakupannya (mencakup ulama, penguasa, tokoh-tokoh sastra, dan lainnya, bukan hanya ulama hadis), kutipan-kutipan sastra yang dilestarikannya, serta dimasukkannya anekdot-anekdot menghibur di samping informasi ilmiah. Kualitas-kualitas ini menjadikannya sama-sama sebuah karya adab (belles-lettres) maupun referensi ilmiah.

Ibn Khallikan wafat di Damaskus pada 681 H (1282 M), meninggalkan kamus biografi yang telah dibaca oleh para ulama dan pembaca terdidik di seluruh dunia Arab sejak penyusunannya.
content_ar: أحمد ibn محمد ﷺ ibn إبراهيم ﷺ ibn Abi Bakr ibn Khallikan was born in Irbil (Erbil in modern Iraq) in 608 AH (1211 CE) into a الأسرة of Kurdish origin that claimed descent from the Barmakid الأسرة of the early Abbasid period. He received an excellent education in his native city and in Mosul قبل traveling to Syria and Egypt, where he eventually settled and pursued a distinguished judicial and العلمي career. Ibn Khallikan served as a الشافعي judge (qadi) in Damascus on multiple occasions — a position he أولاً held under the Ayyubid sultan and later under the early Mamluk regime — and was recognized as one of the foremost legal العلماء of his generation. His personal relationships with major political figures of the era, بما في ذلك the brilliant Ayyubid prince and scholar al-مالك al-Ashraf, gave him access to information and sources that enriched his biographical work. His major work, Wafayat al-A'yan wa Anba Abna az-Zaman (Deaths of Eminent Men and News of the Sons of the Age), universally known as Wafayat al-A'yan or Ibn Khallikan's Biographical Dictionary, is one of the most celebrated biographical dictionaries in Islamic literary history. Composed over many years and completed in 672 AH (1274 CE), it contains entries for approximately 865 notable figures organized alphabetically. Wafayat al-A'yan is distinguished from other Islamic biographical dictionaries by several qualities: the elegant Arabic literary prose in which it is written, the breadth of its scope (بما في ذلك العلماء, rulers, literary figures, and others, not only الحديث العلماء), the literary quotations it preserves, and its inclusion of entertaining anecdotes alongside العلمي information. These qualities made it as much a work of adab (belles-lettres) as a العلمي reference. Ibn Khallikan died in Damascus in 681 AH (1282 CE), leaving behind a biographical dictionary that has been read by العلماء and educated readers across the Arab الدنيا ever بما أن its composition.
